_notes: Keyed by a short operator code (your own convention — GWR, AWC, etc). 2026-07-13: seeded 28 new entries from api/incidents.js's TOC_REGION_TABLE, using that table's own 2-letter ATOC-style codes (VT, GR, XC, ...) as the key — name only, awaiting Wikipedia/manual enrichment like any other entry. RESOLVED: WMR (West Midlands Railway) predates that seed and keeps its own, longer code on purpose — TOC_REGION_TABLE's WM is a real-world ATOC operator code required for matching the live Darwin incident feed in api/incidents.js, not an arbitrary internal choice, so it is NOT changed to WMR for internal consistency; do not add a WM entry here. 2026-07-13 canonicalization pass (see chat): `aliases` (array of strings) is a new manual/curated field — OSM-tagged naming variants (spelling, casing, historical franchise/legal names) that should be treated as the same operator as this entry's `name`; also added to matching stations-content.json entries' `operators` arrays, replacing the variant string with the canonical `name`. `legal_entity` (string) is a separate new manual/curated field for a DIFFERENT real entity behind a brand (e.g. the group company operating it) — deliberately NOT folded into `aliases`/station data, since equating a legal entity with its brand would be wrong, not just imprecise. 2026-07-14: SE (Southeastern)'s wikipedia_title set to "Southeastern (train operating company)" — verified live against Wikipedia's API as a real, non-disambiguation page, exact match. GN (Great Northern)'s wikipedia_title set to "Greater Thameslink Railway", NOT its own article — bare "Great Northern" is a disambiguation page and no "Great Northern (train operating company)" article exists; Wikipedia confirms Thameslink Southern Great Northern Ltd, trading as Greater Thameslink Railway (GTR), took over Govia Thameslink Railway's services on 31 May 2026 and runs Gatwick Express/Great Northern/Southern/Thameslink as sub-brands with no separate per-brand articles post-merger, so GTR's article is genuinely the correct/only source for Great Northern content today. This is a deliberate exception to the usual "wikipedia_title points to the entity's own article" rule — flagged here so it isn't mistaken for a mis-resolution later. 2026-07-14 follow-up: swept SN/TL/GX/GN (the four Greater Thameslink Railway sub-brands) for stale "Govia Thameslink Railway" references. SN's headline/parent_company updated to "Greater Thameslink Railway". TL and GX already had it correctly recorded as history (a franchises entry ending 31 May 2026, TL also has the new "Greater Thameslink Railway" entry starting then) — left untouched, that's accurate history not a stale current-parent field. GN had no Govia reference to begin with (freshly set this session). NOTE left unresolved: TL's parent_company is "Govia" (not "Govia Thameslink Railway") — a different, narrower string referring to the private-sector parent group; didn't touch it since it wasn't the string asked about, but it's arguably equally stale post-nationalization and may need its own follow-up. 2026-07-14: "Southern Railway" folded into SN/Southern as a 19th alias (added to its `aliases` array; 15 stations' operators arrays updated) — after the membership-checking extraction switch this string reappeared as a genuinely stop-verified (not proximity) OSM tag, always co-occurring with "Southern" at the same stations, confirming it's a stale/duplicate relation tag for the same brand rather than the historic 1923–1947 Southern Railway company or proximity noise. By contrast "Greater Thameslink Railway" (which also reappeared under membership-checking, co-occurring with Thameslink/Southern/Gatwick Express/Great Northern) was deliberately NOT folded anywhere — it's the real current legal successor entity (GTR, since 31 May 2026) that Southern/Thameslink/Great Northern/Gatwick Express now operate under as sub-brands, confirmed via its own Wikipedia article; equating it with any one sub-brand would be wrong, same reasoning as `legal_entity`. fleet_classes store the EXACT display label as it should appear (not a bare number + assumed 'Class ' prefix) — this covers cases like Manchester Metrolink's M5000 that don't follow the 'Class NN' pattern. 'id' is used to link through to that vehicle's entry on the database page; 'display' is shown verbatim, no reformatting. franchises is the full history; sidebar should show the current one as a simple 'Operating since {start}' line by default, with the full array available as an expandable detail. FIELD OWNERSHIP (see scripts/ for the population scripts): headline/parent_company/franchises are written by scripts/fetch-wikipedia-facts.mjs (Wikipedia, narrative/historical — Claude extracts, never invents; headline is a punchy 8-12 word sentence, not a paragraph; a franchise entry only appears if the article states its dates). schema_jsonld is written by scripts/build-schema-jsonld.mjs (template assembly from the fields above, no new facts, no API calls). 2026-07-14 UI/content pass (mockup-routes.png/mockup-station.png): `stations_operated` is now computed by scripts/compute-operator-stats.mjs — a plain count of stations-content.json entries listing this operator's `name`/`aliases`, i.e. it inherits fetch-osm-facts.mjs's route-relation membership-verification standard rather than being a fresh guess. A genuine 0 (no verified station found — HT/Hull Trains, HX/Heathrow Express, XR/Elizabeth line as of this run) is left unset rather than written as literal 0, since 0 would misleadingly render as "no stations" for operators that plainly have real coverage; OSM just doesn't have a stop-verified route relation for them under these names yet. `regions_served` (main/other) and `notable_features` are now written by scripts/fetch-wikipedia-facts.mjs, same grounded-extraction discipline as headline/parent_company/franchises (never invented; omitted if the article doesn't state it) — see that script's header. `fleet_classes` remains manual/curated only — extracting specific class numbers from prose carries a higher hallucination risk than the other fields and needs the structured id/display shape below; not automated in this pass. name/wikipedia_title are manual/curated only. _wikipedia is provenance metadata written by the Wikipedia script (no _osm block here — OSM doesn't own any operator-level field in this design; the OSM-derived operator name lives on the station/route entries that reference it, via their own operator/operators fields). 2026-07-13 follow-up: `sub_brands` (array of strings, on LE/Greater Anglia so far) is a named service brand under this operator that intentionally does NOT get its own operators-content.json entry and is NOT folded into `aliases`/station data — distinct from both a naming variant (aliases) and a different legal entity (legal_entity). `welsh_name` (string, on AW so far) is the official bilingual Welsh-language name — kept as its own field rather than merged into `aliases` because it's a live, still-used official name, not a deprecated/historical variant. REVISED 2026-07-19: AW was renamed from "Transport for Wales" to "Transport for Wales Rail" and wikipedia_title re-pointed to match — "Transport for Wales" (Welsh Government's overall multi-modal transport body) and "Transport for Wales Rail" (the actual train operating company, abbr AW in its own Wikipedia infobox) are DIFFERENT real entities with separate Wikipedia articles; the AW entry was previously, incorrectly, sourced from the parent body's article. `welsh_name` updated to "Trafnidiaeth Cymru Trenau" (the rail company's own official Welsh name per its infobox's other_name field, more specific than the general org's plain "Trafnidiaeth Cymru"); "Transport for Wales"/"Trafnidiaeth Cymru"/"Trafnidiaeth Cymru Trenau" are now all aliases. station-content.json's "Trafnidiaeth Cymru" operator tags (238 stations) were renamed to "Transport for Wales Rail", same canonicalization precedent as every other alias-folding pass in this file — the earlier "deliberately left as-is" call is superseded by this correction. website (added 2026-07-19) is the operator's official corporate website URL, written by scripts/fetch-official-websites.mjs — deterministic, no AI (Wikipedia article title -> connected Wikidata item -> its official-website/P856 claim), NOT fetch-wikipedia-facts.mjs: confirmed live that plain-text article extraction cannot see infobox fields like this at all (0/30 operators extracted via that pathway before this script was built). Provenance under _wikidata (fetched_at, qid) — a separate namespace from _wikipedia. map.html's bottom link row shows this as the leftmost "Official website" link, paired with Wikipedia. RESOLVED 2026-07-19 (was flagged as a known gap in the previous note): GW's wikipedia_title was pointing at the HISTORIC 1833-1947 company's article (Q843251, no website claim) — re-pointed to "Great Western Railway (train operating company)" and headline/parent_company/franchises/regions_served/notable_features fully re-extracted from the correct source. Same class of bug found and fixed the same day on AW (see welsh_name note above). BOTH GW and AW's franchises/regions_served/fleet_classes were then further corrected by hand from each article's raw infobox wikitext (Special:Export-equivalent, prop=revisions&rvprop=content), NOT scripts/fetch-wikipedia-facts.mjs's normal Claude/prose extraction — confirmed live that Claude's prose-based extraction produces a plausible-looking but WRONG approximation of these two fields specifically: it extracts the company's narrated trading-name history (e.g. "Great Western Trains" -> "First Great Western" -> ...) as if those were franchises, when the infobox's own `franchise=` parameter actually names the two DfT-designated franchise PERIODS ("InterCity Great Western" 1996-2006, "Greater Western" 2006-2026) — a different, more official categorization entirely invisible to plain-text extraction. `fleet_classes` (previously empty for every operator — "not automated in this pass" per the field's own definition above) was populated for GW/AW only, from the same verified infobox `fleet=` parameter — NOT rolled out to the other 28 operators in this pass; flagged as a good candidate for a dedicated infobox-wikitext extraction script if the same treatment is wanted site-wide, rather than hand-verifying each one. KNOWN DIVERGENCE (not a bug, left as-is both places): GW/AW's Wikipedia-infobox-stated `stationsop` (198 / 248) differs from this file's own `stations_operated` (237 / 64) — the latter is intentionally a live, independently-computed count (scripts/compute-operator-stats.mjs, OSM route-relation membership) rather than a Wikipedia-stated snapshot; the two fields measure different things by design, see that field's own definition above. 2026-07-19 investigation: AW's stations_operated was stuck at 64 (vs Wikipedia's stated 248) even after the Trafnidiaeth Cymru rename above — root cause confirmed live via direct Overpass queries against Cardiff Central/Swansea/Wrexham General's actual route relations: OSM's own operator= tag is near-universally Welsh ("Trafnidiaeth Cymru") or the generic English parent name ("Transport for Wales") — NEVER literally "Transport for Wales Rail" as a raw OSM tag value; that string only exists in this file's own data, from the rename above. The 64 count predated BOTH (a) AW's name/aliases being updated and (b) scripts/compute-operator-stats.mjs being re-run afterward — it was matching only the bare "Transport for Wales" alias, silently excluding every Welsh-tagged relation (the overwhelming majority). This is NOT an OSM data-quality gap — OSM's route-relation coverage for Welsh stations is genuinely complete, just tagged in Welsh. Re-ran scripts/compute-operator-stats.mjs after the alias fix: corrected count is 271 (all of NWP/BNG/PMD/HHD and every other spot-checked Welsh station now match correctly). 271 vs Wikipedia's 248 is the same class of divergence as GW's 237-vs-198 (see that note above) — our count is stations verifiably SERVED (a stop-verified route relation passes through), Wikipedia's stationsop figure is stations MANAGED — not re-investigated further per this pass's scope; flagged for the same planned UI clarification. 2026-07-19 follow-up investigation (triggered by GN/Greater Thameslink Railway stationsop looking wrong: 56 vs Wikipedia's 238): confirmed GN's case is NOT a bug — GTR's own infobox literally states "abbr = GN, GX, SN, TL", i.e. 238 is a combined total across all four sub-brands, not comparable to any single brand's own count (SN+TL+GX+GN sums to 395, exceeding 238, as expected from stations served by multiple overlapping sub-brands). That prompted a full 30-operator sweep for the SAME general risk class (wikipedia_title pointing at the wrong scope/entity), which found FOUR separate, genuine bugs distinct from GN's: GR ("LNER"), SN ("Southern"), GC ("Grand Central"), and IL ("Island Line") all had wikipedia_title pointing at DISAMBIGUATION pages, not real articles — confirmed live via prop=pageprops&ppprop=disambiguation against all 30. Root cause: fetch-wikipedia-facts.mjs only disambiguation-checks a title it auto-resolves itself; an already-set wikipedia_title (however it got there) is trusted as-is and never validated — same root-cause class as the GW/AW wrong-article bugs fixed earlier this session, just a different failure mode (disambiguation vs wrong-but-real article). All four re-pointed to their real, correctly-scoped articles (verified live: each has its own infobox with abbr matching this operator's own key) and fully re-extracted (headline/parent_company/franchises/regions_served/notable_features via Claude from the correct article, website via Wikidata, franchises/regions_served/fleet_classes further corrected by hand from each infobox's raw wikitext, same discipline as GW/AW). GR's notable finding: its old data (headline "LNER name spans a 1923 railway company and a 2018 operator", franchises combining BOTH the historic 1923-1947 London and North Eastern Railway AND the modern 2018-present London North Eastern Railway into one list) was a direct symptom of extracting from the disambiguation page's own text, which literally lists both meanings — a clean example of why this bug class matters, not just a cosmetic mismatch. SEPARATE, NOT fixed this pass — flagged as a genuine judgment call rather than a clear bug: TL (Thameslink) and XR (Elizabeth line) both use {{Infobox rail service}} (a ROUTE/SERVICE infobox — locale/line_used/stock fields, no franchise/stationsop/abbr at all) rather than a company infobox; Thameslink's own infobox states its operator as [[Greater Thameslink Railway]], and no dedicated "Thameslink (brand)"-style modern company article exists (unlike Southern's) — "Thameslink (train operating company)" redirects to a disambiguation page, and the only date-qualified alternative is explicitly the HISTORIC 1997-2006 company. Given no unambiguous single correct company-scoped target exists, TL/XR's current wikipedia_title (the best available service-level article) was left as-is rather than force a guess — flagged for a human decision (accept as best-available, or fold into GN's shared-GTR pattern) rather than silently changed. Confirmed the remaining operators with no abbr/marks param (CH, ME, LD, WR) DO have genuine company-type infoboxes (Infobox rail company / Infobox public transit / Infobox Rail companies / Infobox company respectively), just using a template variant without that specific param — not a scope-mismatch signal, no further action needed. CONCLUSION on "should we double check all other operators again": the systematic risk class here is DISAMBIGUATION-PAGE wikipedia_title values specifically (now fully swept and fixed, 0 remaining) — remaining stationsop numeric differences across operators are understood as either the already-documented "served vs managed" methodology difference (GW-style) or genuine shared/different-entity scope (GN-style), not further undetected bugs. 2026-07-18 follow-up (resolving the TL/XR judgment call flagged above): confirmed live via Wikipedia's raw infobox wikitext (prop=revisions&rvprop=content, not prose extraction) for both. TL (Thameslink): its {{Infobox rail service}} states operator = [[Greater Thameslink Railway]], cited to a 26 September 2025 DfT announcement, and its predecessor field lists only historic entities (Thameslink train operating company 1997-2006, First Capital Connect, Govia Thameslink Railway 2014-2026) — confirming no modern standalone "Thameslink" company article exists, same underlying pattern as GN (a Greater Thameslink Railway sub-brand with no dedicated company-scoped article of its own). Unlike GN, TL's wikipedia_title did not need to change: "Thameslink" is a real, non-disambiguation article (about the service/route) and remains the correct best-available target, it was simply never a company infobox to begin with. XR (Elizabeth line): checked independently rather than assumed symmetric, since its operator has changed over time — its infobox states operator = [[GTS Rail Operations]] (under concession from [[TfL]]), cited to a 19 November 2024 TfL press release announcing GTS Rail Operations (a Go-Ahead Group/Tokyo Metro/Sumitomo Corporation joint venture) as the line's new operator, superseding the historic MTR Elizabeth line/MTR Corporation arrangement (already correctly reflected in this entry's own franchises array). XR does NOT match the GN/TL pattern: GTS Rail Operations is a genuine, distinct operating entity under a TfL concession, not a case of a brand with no dedicated article borrowing a shared parent's. No field changes needed for either entry — TL's parent_company/franchises and XR's parent_company already correctly stated GTR and GTS Rail Operations respectively before this check; this note exists purely to close out the flagged judgment call. 2026-07-18 follow-up: standardized TL/SN/GX's parent_company to match GN's phrasing — "Thameslink Southern Great Northern Limited (Greater Thameslink Railway), owned by DfT Operator" — confirmed against GTR's own Wikipedia infobox/prose (parent_company = [[DfT Operator]]; prose states GTR "is a state-owned British train operating company that took over the services of privately-owned operator Govia Thameslink Railway on 31 May 2026"). TL's parent_company had been stale since the 2026-07-14 sweep (still read "Govia Thameslink Railway", the pre-nationalisation private predecessor, contradicting its own franchises array which already correctly listed GTR as current since 31 May 2026) — this was flagged then as needing its own follow-up and is now fixed. SN/GX previously read the bare trade name "Greater Thameslink Railway" (correct owner, but missing the state-ownership/DfT Operator detail GN's entry already had) — brought in line for consistency across all four sub-brands, since all four share the exact same current parent entity. 2026-07-18: fleet_classes rollout extended from GW/AW (the only two previously populated, per the earlier note) to all remaining operators except GN and WR, hand-verified from each operator's own Wikipedia infobox raw wikitext (fleet=/stock= parameter, prop=revisions&rvprop=content — same discipline as GW/AW, not scripts/fetch-wikipedia-facts.mjs's prose extraction). WMR and LN share one underlying article ("West Midlands Trains", the operating company) whose infobox lists fleet per-brand (a 'West Midlands Railway' list and a separate 'London Northwestern Railway' list under the same fleet= parameter) — split accordingly rather than giving both entries the combined list, matching this file's usual per-entry precision. CH's Class 01/5 (a shunter sub-class) and CH/CS's Mark 5A/Mark 5 carriages are non-'Class N' rolling stock named exactly as the infobox states them, not forced into a 'Class NN' template — same precedent as the M5000 case already noted for this field. ES (Eurostar)'s fleet mixes British Rail-style classes (e300/e320, displayed as "Class 373 (e300)"/"Class 374 (e320)" since both namings are in real use) with non-British-Rail SNCF/Thalys TGV variants (PBA, PBKA) that have no Class number at all — given their own exact labels rather than invented ones. TWO DELIBERATE EXCEPTIONS, left unset rather than guessed: GN (Great Northern) — its wikipedia_title is GTR's own article (no dedicated Great Northern article exists, see this file's earlier GN note), and GTR's infobox fleet= is a COMBINED list across all four sub-brands (171/377/379/387/700/717) with no per-brand breakdown stated anywhere; assigning that combined list to GN specifically would misattribute rolling stock actually run under the Southern/Thameslink/Gatwick Express brands as if it were Great Northern's own fleet, so it's left unset rather than invented — same 'don't write a misleading number' reasoning already used for the stations_operated field (HT/HX/XR left unset rather than a literal 0). WR (West Coast Railways) — uses a generic {{Infobox company}} (it's a charter/spot-hire operator, not a scheduled TOC), which has no fleet parameter at all; its actual locomotive roster is only ever mentioned in prose and changes over time as a spot-hire business, so there's no single grounded infobox fact to extract.

WMR.name: West Midlands Railway
WMR.headline: West Midlands Railway returned to state ownership on 1 February 2026

franchises.name: Transport UK Group and Mitsui & Co (West Midlands Trains)
WM Trains Limited (West Midlands Railway / London Northwestern Railway)

WMR.legal_entity: West Midlands Trains (parent legal entity; also covers the London Northwestern Railway brand — not folded in as a simple alias)
notable_features: Operates under two trading names: West Midlands Railway and London Northwestern Railway
Became state-owned on 1 February 2026 following the end of its outsourcing contract with Transport UK Group and Mitsui & Co
Services on the Stourbridge Town branch line are run by open access operator Pre Metro Operations on its behalf

VT.name: Avanti West Coast
VT.headline: Avanti West Coast posted Britain's worst train cancellation rate in 2022
notable_features: Operates the West Coast Partnership franchise, successor to Virgin Trains' InterCity West Coast
Inherited 56 Class 390 Pendolino tilting high-speed EMUs and 20 Class 221 Super Voyager DEMUs from Virgin Trains
Pendolino fleet underwent major refurbishment between 2021 and 2024
Replaced Super Voyagers in 2024 with new Class 805 bi-mode and Class 807 electric multiple units from the Hitachi A-train family
New Class 805/807 units do not tilt unlike the Pendolinos and Super Voyagers they replaced
Had the worst train cancellation record in Great Britain (8% vs 3.8% national average) in year to November 2022
Launched smartcard ticketing in February 2021 as an alternative to paper tickets
Orange triangle logo represents the three geographic points of the West Coast Main Line
Being nationalised, with routes transferring to Great British Railways by October 2027
Set to be one of the last two train operators nationalised in Great Britain

GR.name: LNER
GR.headline: State rescue operator LNER now runs East Coast trains after Virgin Trains collapse
notable_features: Named after the historic London and North Eastern Railway, one of the Big Four (1923–1948)
State-owned operator of last resort, taking over from Virgin Trains East Coast in 2018
Direct award made it the longest franchise on the East Coast Main Line since GNER
Introduced Hitachi-built Class 800/801 'Azuma' bi-mode and electric trains from 2019
Aiming for a 4-hour London–Edinburgh run, a feat previously achieved only once daily by the Flying Scotsman
Pioneered fares reform, abolishing most return fares and trialling a '70-minute flex' ticket
Leading rollout of ERTMS digital in-cab signalling on the East Coast Main Line
Ordered Class 897 'Serenza' tri-mode trains, expected to be the first new trains under Great British Railways
Suffered a stabbing attack on a London-bound Azuma service near Huntingdon in November 2025

XC.name: CrossCountry
XC.headline: CrossCountry once ran Britain's longest direct rail journey until 2025
notable_features: Once operated the UK's longest direct rail passenger service, Aberdeen to Penzance (13h15m), ended May 2025
One of only two franchised UK train operators that manages no stations (the other being Caledonian Sleeper)
Operates diesel trains only, as none of its routes are fully electrified
Removed the tilt function from its Class 221 Super Voyagers in 2008 to improve reliability
Network is centred on Birmingham New Street and deliberately avoids Greater London

EM.name: East Midlands Railway
EM.headline: East Midlands Railway set to return to public ownership by 2026
notable_features: Ordered 33 new bi-mode Class 810 Aurora high-speed train sets
Leased four ex-Hull Trains Class 180 units despite their history of technical difficulties
Contactless payment accepted between London St Pancras and Luton Airport Parkway
Operates Luton Airport Express service linked to automated Luton DART people mover, cutting journey to 32 minutes
Workers took part in the first national rail strike in the UK for three decades (2022–2023)
Ran some of the last regular InterCity 125 (Class 43) services, inherited then replaced by ex-LNER sets

LN.name: London Northwestern Railway
LN.headline: State-owned West Midlands Trains took over London Northwestern Railway in February 2026
LN.parent_company: WM Trains Limited (trading as West Midlands Trains)
notable_features: Operates commuter services along the West Coast Main Line out of London Euston
Runs two local branch lines splitting off the WCML at Watford Junction and Bletchley
Express trains on its WCML corridor are operated by Avanti West Coast, not LNR itself
Brand taken over by state-owned WM Trains Limited on 1 February 2026, ending the Transport UK Group/Mitsui & Co contract

GW.name: Great Western Railway
GW.headline: GWR's battery train set a 200.5-mile world distance record
notable_features: Operated the largest InterCity 125 (HST) fleet in Britain
Runs the Night Riviera sleeper between London and Penzance, once including the UK's last Motorail service
Class 230 battery train set a record of 200.5 miles on a single charge, the longest for any BEMU
Operates the Heathrow Express service and serves Gatwick via the North Downs Line
Introduced Class 800/802 Intercity Express Trains, gradually replacing the InterCity 125 fleet from 2017
Still offers Pullman Dining restaurant cars on some West Country and Wales services
Manages 198 stations across a 1,997 km network carrying 89 million passenger journeys a year

SW.name: South Western Railway
SW.headline: South Western Railway returns to state ownership after FirstGroup-MTR franchise ends
notable_features: State-owned train operator after nationalisation of the FirstGroup/MTR franchise in May 2025
Operates the Island Line brand on the Isle of Wight using Class 484 units converted from London Underground D78 Stock
Most services use 750 V DC third rail electrification, with a diesel fleet retained for the unelectrified West of England line
Bans non-folding bicycles from peak-time trains in a defined London commuter zone to save space
Retired its Class 455 fleet in March 2026 following introduction of Class 701 Arterio units

SE.name: Southeastern
SE.headline: Southeastern was renationalised in 2021 after a £25 million revenue fraud scandal
notable_features: Operates the domestic services on High Speed 1
State-owned operator of last resort, taking over after a £25 million revenue declaration fraud
Network spans 540 miles (870 km) covering 180 stations, 164 of which it manages
£27 million refurbishment of its 29 Class 395 Javelin high-speed trains
£10 million upgrade of its 112-strong Class 375 Electrostar fleet
First operator to ban e-scooters and electric skateboards over lithium-ion battery fire risk
Introduced digital 'sTickets' season tickets bought via smartphone
Developing a fully automated vehicle inspection system with Siemens Mobility and Eversholt Rail, first deployed at Ramsgate depot

SN.name: Southern
SN.headline: Southern topped Which?'s worst-rated rail operator list three years running
SN.parent_company: Thameslink Southern Great Northern Limited (Greater Thameslink Railway), owned by DfT Operator

franchises.name: Part of Thameslink, Southern and Great Northern

notable_features: Ranked last for passenger satisfaction by Which? for three consecutive years, 2016–2018
Ranked second-worst on overall passenger satisfaction by Transport Focus in 2022
First rail company in England to use the ITSO smartcard system, in 2012
Only 20% of trains arrived on time between April 2015 and March 2016
Government paid Southern £50 million to cover costs of 2016 strike disruption
Fined passengers for standing in first class on an overcrowded train in 2015
Doubled the number of London to Brighton express trains after absorbing Gatwick Express in 2007–2008

TL.name: Thameslink
TL.headline: Thameslink's £7bn upgrade introduced world-first automatic train operation over ETCS Level 2
TL.parent_company: Thameslink Southern Great Northern Limited (Greater Thameslink Railway), owned by DfT Operator
notable_features: World-first Automatic Train Operation (ATO) over ETCS Level 2 in the core section
Reopened the Snow Hill tunnel to passengers after 72 years in 1988
Part of the route (Bedford to Three Bridges) runs 24 hours a day
Subject of the £7 billion Thameslink Programme infrastructure upgrade
Uses purpose-built Class 700 dual-voltage trains introduced in 2016
Passenger demand quadrupled initial projections after the 1988 launch

GX.name: Gatwick Express
GX.headline: First British Rail InterCity sector spun off as a private franchise
GX.parent_company: Thameslink Southern Great Northern Limited (Greater Thameslink Railway), owned by DfT Operator
notable_features: First portion of British Rail's InterCity sector converted into a separate train operating unit ahead of privatisation
Launched as a non-stop London-to-airport express service in May 1984
Among the first UK rail journeys to accept Oyster and contactless payment cards, from January 2016
Class 387/2 fleet allows three units to couple into a 12-coach train, versus a 10-coach maximum for the Class 442s it replaced
ASLEF drivers staged a dispute over Driver-Only Operation on the new 12-car Class 387 trains
Ordered by the Advertising Standards Authority in 2018 to drop its 30-minute journey time claim due to poor performance

GN.name: Great Northern
GN.headline: Great Northern's Moorgate route could be absorbed into London Overground by 2027
GN.parent_company: Thameslink Southern Great Northern Limited (Greater Thameslink Railway), owned by DfT Operator
notable_features: Runs suburban services on the southern end of the East Coast Main Line
TfL has proposed taking over Moorgate to Welwyn Garden City, Hertford North and Stevenage services and integrating them into London Overground, potentially from Autumn 2027
Proposed takeover would increase frequency from 2 to 4 trains per hour and standardise fares
Proposed change linked to a planned 21,000-home development at Crews Hill
Any Overground takeover would continue to use the existing Class 717 rolling stock

CC.name: c2c
CC.headline: State-owned DfT Operator takes over c2c's East London-Essex rail services in July 2025
notable_features: Class 357 and Class 720 trains capable of 100mph but restricted to 75mph by the line's speed limit
Fastest timetabled journey between Fenchurch Street and Shoeburyness is 56 minutes
Entire fleet inherited from predecessor operator, maintained at East Ham and Shoeburyness depots

CH.name: Chiltern Railways
CH.headline: Only UK rail franchise once 51% owned by its own directors
notable_features: First post-privatisation franchise to order new rolling stock (Class 168 Clubman DMUs)
Uniquely had 51% of its founding shares controlled by its own directors
Shares tracks with London Underground's Metropolitan line under a unique unregulated track-access agreement
Runs a parliamentary train service on the Cherwell Valley line purely to maintain driver route knowledge
Suffered a six-week main line closure after the Gerrards Cross Tunnel collapse in 2005, leading to Tesco compensation
Delivered £250 million Evergreen 3 upgrade enabling 100 mph running on the Chiltern Main Line
Operates peak-hour locomotive-hauled services using Class 67/68 locomotives with Mark 3 coaches
Converted a Class 168 unit into 'HybridFlex', a battery hybrid train
PPM regularly exceeded 90%, rated among Britain's best-performing operators in the mid-2010s
Set to return to public ownership in September 2026 under Great British Railways reforms

LE.name: Greater Anglia
LE.headline: State-owned Greater Anglia took over Essex-to-Norfolk services in October 2025
notable_features: Operates the Stansted Express airport rail link between Stansted Airport and London Liverpool Street
Installed new anti-trespass measures including fences, gates and 'witches' hats' after trespass incidents caused over 18 days of delays in 2025/26
State-owned, having taken over from Transport UK Group and Mitsui & Co under the Passenger Railway Services (Public Ownership) Act 2024

NT.name: Northern
NT.headline: First rail franchise stripped for poor performance since Connex in 2003
notable_features: First train operating company to have its franchise terminated for poor performance since Connex South Eastern in 2003
Now run directly by the government's operator of last resort (DfT Operator)
Introduced new Class 195 diesel and Class 331 electric multiple units fully into service by December 2020
Affected by the 2022–2024 UK railway strikes, the largest national rail strikes in three decades
Operates a rare parliamentary train service on the Stockport–Stalybridge line, twice weekly on Saturdays
Plans to procure up to 386 new trains totalling 896 carriages, introduced from 2030

TP.name: TransPennine Express
TP.headline: State-run TransPennine Express restored Castleford-York trains after a 50-year gap
notable_features: Operated by the government as operator of last resort after predecessor's poor performance
Reinstated direct services between Castleford and York for the first time in 50 years
Ranked among the least punctual UK train operators in 2023, with only 47.7% on-time performance
Generated over 311,000 delay compensation claims in a single year
Ran Nova 3 sets of Class 68 diesel locomotives hauling Mark 5A carriages, later withdrawn
Planning procurement of up to 55 new trains (330 carriages) to support the Transpennine Route Upgrade

ME.name: Merseyrail
ME.headline: Merseyrail once achieved the UK's highest-ever train reliability score
ME.parent_company: Serco and Transport UK Group (50:50 joint venture, via Merseyrail Electrics 2002 Limited)
notable_features: Uses Class 777 trains based on the Stadler METRO platform
Battery-electric (BEMU) trains reach Headbolt Lane despite unelectrified track
Carried 29.9 million passengers in 2024/2025
Network largely third-rail and self-contained/isolated from the rest of National Rail
76 miles of routes, of which 6.5 miles are underground
Named UK's most reliable train operator in 2010 with 96.33% reliability, the highest ever recorded by a UK operator
Mersey Railway Tunnel was among the first underground railway tunnels
Won a 2025 National Rail Award in the 'Great Place to Work' category
Achieved 93% overall passenger satisfaction in the 2025 Transport Focus survey

SR.name: ScotRail
SR.headline: Scotland's railway renationalised in 2022 after Abellio's franchise failed
notable_features: State-owned operator of last resort after Abellio franchise was terminated
Permanently scrapped peak fares from September 2025, cutting commuter costs by up to 50%
Introduced AI-generated announcer voices, including one based on a ScotRail employee
Operates 'Highland Explorer' carriages with a 50:50 seating-to-bike-rack split
Runs 355 stations across Scotland
Electrifying the Fife Circle line for battery electric multiple unit use
Leasing 22 Class 222 units to replace its Inter7City fleet from 2027
Introduced a £10 minimum fare for passengers without valid tickets to tackle fare evasion

CS.name: Caledonian Sleeper
CS.headline: Caledonian Sleeper: one of only two remaining UK overnight sleeper services
notable_features: One of only two currently operating sleeper services in the UK, the other being the Night Riviera
Uses a combination of Class 92 electric locomotives and rebuilt Class 73/9 electro-diesel locomotives
New Mark 5 carriage fleet introduced during 2019, replacing British Rail-era rolling stock
Serco pledged to invest £100 million in new rolling stock as part of its 2015 franchise bid
Trains can run at up to 100 mph if delayed by more than 20 minutes, above their normal 80 mph limit
From January 2026 the Highland Sleeper will also call at Birmingham International

GC.name: Grand Central
GC.headline: Open-access operator's rights extended to run trains until December 2038
notable_features: Operates as an open-access operator rather than under a franchise agreement
Not eligible for the same government financial support as franchised operators during COVID-19
Named individual trains 'The Zephyr' and 'The 21st Century Limited' on the 2008 timetable
Operated overhauled InterCity 125 High Speed Trains with Class 43 power cars
Ordered nine Hitachi-built bi-mode/battery trains (Class 820) to replace ageing Class 180 fleet
Granted operating rights extension by the ORR in March 2025 lasting until December 2038
Proposed unprecedented mid-route train splitting on the East Coast Main Line for a Cleethorpes service

HT.name: Hull Trains
HT.headline: Open-access Hull Trains named Best UK Train Operator with 97% satisfaction
notable_features: Established in 1999 as an open-access operator in response to low InterCity East Coast service frequency
Holds a track-access agreement extended multiple times, now running until December 2032
Named Best UK Train Operator in 2017 with a 97% passenger satisfaction rate
Introduction of Class 222 Pioneers raised top speed from 100 mph to 125 mph
Operates a fleet of five Class 802 'Paragon' bi-mode multiple units introduced from December 2019
Repeatedly suspended and resumed services during the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020 and 2021

LD.name: Lumo
LD.headline: Open-access rail operator Lumo offers only standard class, no guards
LD.legal_entity: GTS Rail Operations (legal operating company behind the Lumo brand, not a separate rebrand)
notable_features: Open-access operator distinct from franchised services, using additional East Coast Main Line paths
Third open-access operator on the East Coast Main Line, after Hull Trains and Grand Central
Offers only standard class seating and does not use guards on its trains
Operates Class 803 electric trains, a Hitachi AT300 variant without a diesel engine, using batteries instead for onboard power during overhead line failures
Fastest East Coast service runs Edinburgh to London in 4 hours and 3 minutes
Brand name 'Lumo' combines 'luminosity' and 'motion'
Promotes itself as a 'digital-first' operator with paperless ticketing

franchises.name: East Coast Main Line open-access track access agreement

HX.name: Heathrow Express
HX.headline: Heathrow Express races non-stop from Paddington to Heathrow in just 15 minutes

franchises.name: Great Western Railway operation under management contract

notable_features: Non-stop 15-minute journey between London Paddington and Heathrow Airport
Built via a joint venture between BAA and British Rail, with construction starting in 1993
Tunnel collapse in 1994 led to record £1.7 million fine for Balfour Beatty and Geoconsult under health and safety legislation
Offered a Paddington check-in and luggage drop-off service (1999-2003), similar to Hong Kong's Airport Express
Brand, uniforms, train design and station architecture created by Wolff Olins
Uses Automatic Train Protection (ATP) and European Train Control System (ETCS) on its route
Over 100 million passengers carried since opening in 1998
New Class 387 Electrostar trains introduced from December 2020, replacing original Class 332 fleet used since 1998
Operates jointly by Great Western Railway and Heathrow Express Operating Company under a contract extended to 2028

XR.name: Elizabeth line
XR.headline: New London railway named for the Queen carries a seventh of UK rail trips
XR.parent_company: Go-Ahead Group, Tokyo Metro and Sumitomo Corporation (GTS Rail Operations)
notable_features: Named after Queen Elizabeth II, who officially opened it during her Platinum Jubilee
Reached over 200 million trips annually in its second year of operation
Carries one seventh of all rail trips in the United Kingdom
Operates exclusively with purpose-built nine-car Class 345 trains carrying up to 1,500 passengers
All 41 stations are step-free from street to platform
Central section runs up to 24 trains per hour in each direction
Won the RIBA Stirling Prize for architecture in 2024
Uses Communications-Based Train Control in the central section and Abbey Wood branch, with ETCS on the Heathrow branch

AW.name: Transport for Wales Rail
AW.headline: Welsh state operator runs unique battery-electric tram-trains on street and rail
notable_features: Manages 248 National Rail stations, including all 223 in Wales
First UK-based train operating company to partner with Payzone for ticket sales
First British train operator outside London to deploy contactless EMV services
Operates Class 398 battery-electric tram-trains able to run on both street-level tracks and conventional railway
Delivering the South Wales Metro, including a new depot at Taff's Well
Took over as operator of last resort in 2021 after KeolisAmey Wales franchise collapsed

IL.name: Island Line
IL.headline: Isle of Wight's Island Line ran ex-London Underground trains for decades
notable_features: Was the only passenger franchise to also include maintenance of the infrastructure
Was the smallest train operating company on the National Rail network until 2007
Uses refurbished ex-London Underground tube trains (Class 483 and Class 484) due to low ceiling in Ryde Tunnel
South West Trains bought the rolling stock outright from HSBC Rail for a nominal £1 in 2007
Stations and trains repainted in a heritage cream and green colour scheme

WR.name: West Coast Railways
WR.headline: First privately owned company ever granted a UK train operating licence
notable_features: Became the first privately owned company to be granted a UK train operating licence, in June 1998
Owned and operated steam locomotive 5972 Olton Hall as 'Hogwarts Castle' hauling the Hogwarts Express in the Harry Potter films
In 2015 became subject to an unprecedented total network access ban by Network Rail, the first of its kind since privatisation
Fined £200,000 plus £64,000 costs after a 2015 signal-passed-at-danger incident at Wootton Bassett Junction
Operates The Jacobite steam service on the West Highland Line, crossing the famous Glenfinnan Viaduct
Repeatedly subject to ORR prohibition notices and exemption revocations over safety and rolling stock issues (2016, 2023, 2024)

ES.name: Eurostar
ES.headline: Eurostar's Channel Tunnel trains carried a record 20 million passengers in 2025
notable_features: Runs through the Channel Tunnel, the world's longest underwater tunnel
Set a British speed record of 334.7 km/h in 2003
First Eurostar extension to a new country with direct London–Amsterdam/Rotterdam services launched in 2018
Formed via 2023 merger with Thalys to create a single Western European high-speed network
Class 373 trains split into two independent half-sets for Channel Tunnel emergency safety
e320 Siemens Velaro trains run at up to 320 km/h and are quadri-current for cross-border operation
Upcoming Eurostar Celestia double-decker trains would be the first major double-decker fleet in the UK
Set record for longest non-stop high-speed journey (London to Cannes, 1,421 km) in 2006
Has run 'Tread Lightly' programme since 2007 aiming for carbon-neutral operation

Bakerloo.name: Bakerloo line
Bakerloo.headline: Brown line linking Harrow & Wealdstone to Elephant & Castle via the West End, opened 1906
notable_features: 25 stations over 23.2 km, of which 15 are underground
Runs partly on the surface and partly through deep-level tube tunnels
One of the original deep-tube lines, opened 10 March 1906

Central.name: Central line
Central.headline: The network's longest line — 49 stations over 74 km, from Ealing/West Ruislip to Epping/Woodford
notable_features: Longest line on the Underground network at 74 km (46 miles)
One of only two lines that cross the Greater London boundary, with the Metropolitan line
Deep-level tube line — trains are smaller than British main-line stock

Circle.name: Circle line
Circle.headline: A spiral, not a circle — loops from Hammersmith around central London and back to Edgware Road
notable_features: 36 stations over 27 km, serving most of London's main-line termini
Track and stations almost entirely shared with the District, Hammersmith & City and Metropolitan lines
Formed a complete loop in 1884; the current spiral shape dates from 2009

District.name: District line
District.headline: London's most branched line — six termini across Upminster, Wimbledon, Kensington, Richmond and Ealing Broadway
notable_features: 60 stations over 64 km — one of the network's largest lines
Splits into multiple branches at Earl's Court and again at Turnham Green
Opened 24 December 1868, one of the original Metropolitan District Railway lines

Hammersmith & City.name: Hammersmith & City line
Hammersmith & City.headline: Pink line from Hammersmith to Barking, skirting the City of London between Farringdon and Aldgate East
notable_features: 29 stations over 25.5 km; shallow-tunnel construction, similar gauge to main-line trains
Almost all track and stations shared with the Circle, District and Metropolitan lines
Over 141 million passenger journeys a year, combined with the Circle line

Jubilee.name: Jubilee line
Jubilee.headline: The newest Underground line — opened 1979, though some track dates back to 1932
notable_features: 27 stations over 36.2 km, from Stanmore to Stratford via the West End and Docklands
Newest line on the network — opened 1 May 1979
Some sections of track date back to 1932 and some stations to 1879

Metropolitan.name: Metropolitan line
Metropolitan.headline: The 'Met' — London's most far-flung line, reaching Amersham and Chesham in Buckinghamshire
notable_features: 34 stations over 66.7 km, with branches to Watford and Uxbridge
Mostly shallow cut-and-cover tunnels between Aldgate and Finchley Road; above ground beyond that
Almost 94 million passenger journeys in 2019

Northern.name: Northern line
Northern.headline: The busiest Underground line — around 340 million journeys a year, with two routes through central London
notable_features: 52 stations over 58 km — busiest line on the network (about 340 million journeys in 2019)
Only line with two separate routes through central London (via Bank and via Charing Cross)
Despite the name, serves the network's southernmost station, Morden

Piccadilly.name: Piccadilly line
Piccadilly.headline: Heathrow's Tube link — 53 stations from Uxbridge/Heathrow to Cockfosters via central London
notable_features: 53 stations over 74 km — serves all Heathrow Airport terminals
Splits into two western branches at Acton Town (Heathrow and Uxbridge)
Sixth-busiest line on the network — nearly 218 million journeys in 2019

Victoria.name: Victoria line
Victoria.headline: One of only two fully-underground lines, linking Brixton to Walthamstow Central
notable_features: 16 stations over 21 km, from Brixton to Walthamstow Central via the West End
One of only two Underground lines that run completely underground, with the Waterloo & City line
Opened 1 September 1968

Waterloo & City.name: Waterloo & City line
Waterloo & City.headline: 'The Drain' — a two-station shuttle between Waterloo and Bank for City commuters
notable_features: Just 2 stations, Waterloo and Bank, with no intermediate stops
Weekdays only — historically closed Sundays and public holidays
The only entirely underground tube line, including its depot (unlike the Victoria line's ground-level depot)
